amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: The General Amino Acid Control (GAAC) responds to amino acid starvation via Gcn4 transcription factor. Under starvation, uncharged tRNAs accumulate, activating Gcn2 kinase which phosphorylates eIF2α. This reduces general translation but paradoxically increases GCN4 translation via upstream ORFs (uORFs) in its 5'UTR. Gcn4 activates >500 genes including amino acid biosynthesis, purine synthesis, autophagy, and mitochondrial function. The system shows remarkable translational control through 4 uORFs that act as ribosome scanning barriers.

name: Gcn4 Amino Acid Starvation Response (GAAC)
notes: Shows elegant translational control via uORFs. Conserved as ISR (Integrated Stress Response) in mammals with 4 eIF2α kinases.
scientificAccuracy: Based on extensive research on yeast GAAC and translational control. Conserved as ISR in mammals.

sources.title: The yeast GCN4 transcriptional activator is translated in response to amino acid starvation by a mechanism involving reinitiation on upstream open reading frames
sources.authors: Thireos G, Penn MD, Hinnebusch AG

sources.title: Phosphorylation of the alpha subunit of eukaryotic initiation factor 2 by protein kinase GCN2 in yeast cells starved for amino acids
sources.authors: Dever TE, Feng L, Wek RC, Cigan AM, Donahue TF, Hinnebusch AG

sources.title: Translational Control of GCN4 by Upstream Open Reading Frames: A Paradigm for Translational Control by Phosphorylation of eIF2α
